# ​1​ Background {#​1​ background}

The Nectar Research Cloud aims to provide cost-effective, scalable and flexible delivery of computational infrastructure, software and services to the Australian research community, enabling them to store, access, use, and share computational models, tools, data, and collaboration environments. One of the key aims of the Research Cloud is to avoid the problems of institutional firewalls, accounts, and access restrictions, in order to enable easier multi-institutional, national, and international collaboration in data-intensive research.

The Nectar Research Cloud is based on OpenStack, the most commonly used open source cloud software, and other open source software.

The Australian Research Data Commons (ARDC) runs the Nectar Research Cloud in collaboration with its partner organisations, known as Nodes. ARDC is funded by the federal government’s National Collaborative Research Infrastructure Strategy (NCRIS).

The Nectar Research Cloud consists of *Federated Operations* and *Node Operations*.

**Federated Operations** are the centrally managed activities, operations, policies, standards, and processes that create a national cloud infrastructure rather than a loose collection of separate cloud systems operated independently by different organisations. The Federated Operations specify and support the national standards and processes for cloud services, and reduce costs and overheads by operating the central cloud services (or “core services”) that the cloud relies on (e.g. authentication, allocations, dashboard/web portal, APIs), so that each Node or organisation does not have to run their own instance of these services.

For the Nectar Research Cloud, Federated Operations are split into two components:

* **Nectar Cloud Services** \- a team of technical staff that implement and operate the central cloud services, provide a pool of world-class expertise in these services, ensure that the cloud services keep up with technological advances and best practice operations, assist Nodes with operations.
* **Nectar User Support** \- operates a national helpdesk for the research cloud, and (in collaboration with the Nodes) provides user support, online user guides, and training material.

**Node Operations** are the activities of the Nodes to host, operate and support the main cloud infrastructure (the servers and storage) and associated cloud services, which operate together with the core services to make up the Nectar Research Cloud infrastructure and services.

The required architecture and infrastructure specifications for a Node (or Site) to join the Nectar Research Cloud federation are specified in this document (Nodes are referred to as Sites in this document, and in user support material for the Research Cloud, to avoid confusion with compute nodes or servers in the cloud infrastructure).

Operational requirements for Federated Operations and Node Operations in the Nectar Research Cloud are specified in a separate document, the [Operations Level Agreement (OLA)](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qu8OOz3vp1m0r6mtyvV39rDQxAJ89t2OCfPWp9CtS2w/edit?usp=sharing), which refers to additional documents describing relevant policies and processes.

## 1.1 Changes from V2 {#1.1-changes-from-v2}

* Rename Management network to Service network
* Rename admin public network to endpoint network
* Removed mention of linuxbridge

# ​2​ Infrastructure {#​2​ infrastructure}

## ​2.1​ Introduction  {#​2.1​ introduction}

This section specifies the requirements for compute and storage infrastructure and services that a Site needs to run to be part of the Nectar Research Cloud.

A *Site* is an organisation that provides cloud infrastructure as part of the Nectar Cloud. Each Site runs one or more *Availability Zones* in the cloud federation.

An *Availability Zone* (AZ) is a logical partition of compute nodes, volume storage, and networking services.  Partitioning the cloud into independent AZs allows users to build their infrastructure in a redundant way across multiple AZs. The main reason a site would have multiple availability zones is when they have multiple data centres, with an AZ equating to a data centre.

Each availability zone consists of some control services and some compute and storage resources. All control services are designed and built to be fully redundant. Compute nodes however are not designed to be redundant, and a failure will impact all virtual machine instances on that node. All storage that is shared is expected to be fault tolerant using best practices.

Sites may have a mix of ARDC-funded infrastructure that can only be used for supporting nationally prioritised resource allocations (*national allocations*) and locally funded infrastructure for supporting locally prioritised resource allocations (*local allocations*). These could be presented as separate AZs.

![][image1]

## ​2.2​ Required Services {#​2.2​ required-services}

Below are the minimum services that are required for a Site to be part of the Nectar Research Cloud.

### ​2.2.1​ Compute Service {#​2.2.1​ compute-service}

The Nectar Compute service provides virtual machines to users on demand. This is provided by the Nova OpenStack Project and components are split between Core Services and each Site in the Nectar federation.

![][image2]

Each Nectar Site runs one or more nova compute cells. A *compute cell* is an internal mechanism (i.e. hidden from the users) that allows the Nectar Cloud to scale in terms of the number of compute nodes and also be geographically disbursed. Each compute cell can conservatively support up to 200 compute nodes, this can be increased with more effort on tuning and scaling the control infrastructure. Compute cells should only be used as a mechanism to scale within a Site.

Each compute cell can provide one or more availability zones. Usually it consists of one. It is also acceptable for multiple compute cells to make up a single availability zone.

Site Components (per compute cell):

* Conductor \- Manages all requests from core services, this is a private internal service
* Novnc \- Provides users a VNC console to their virtual machines. This service should be publicly accessible
* Compute \- Runs on each compute node and interfaces with the hypervisor
* MQ \- Message queue
* DB \- Relational database

Sites should support the latest versions of the standard Nectar flavours (VM instance sizes), and can also support their own custom flavours.

Access to the novnc service is via the User Proxies (more info in that section)

### ​2.2.2​ Image Service {#​2.2.2​ image-service}

The Nectar Image service uses the OpenStack Glance project and supports discovering, registering, and retrieving virtual machine (VM) images. It does not store the actual images, these are stored in the Nectar Object Store service.

![][image3]
Site Components:

* API \- This is an internal service that should be accessible by other internal OpenStack services including compute and volume within the Site.

The Site level API service provides a local cache for images. Sites should have at least 2 API servers for redundancy.

Access to the glance api service is via the User Proxies (more info in that section)

### ​2.2.3​ Volume Service {#​2.2.3​ volume-service}

The Nectar Volume service provides block devices to virtual machines, this is provided by the Cinder OpenStack project.
![][image4]
Site components:

* Volume \- handles requests from users and interfaces with storage and compute nodes
* Backup \- handles backup requests for volumes hosted at the Site. Backups are usually stored in the Nectar Object Store Service.

Each volume server can manage multiple backends, alternatively you can deploy a separate volume server per storage backend. You must have one volume availability zone per compute availability zone.

### ​2.2.4​ Networking Service {#​2.2.4​ networking-service}

The Networking Service allows users to create virtual private networks, routers, floating IPs firewalls and load balancers. It is also responsible for binding operator provided networks to instances. This service is provided by the Neutron openstack project and the Software Defined Networking (SDN) part is provided by OVN.

Each site must have at least 2 network nodes and these can service multiple compute cells.

Existing sites will have linuxbridge components however new sites will only have OVN components.

Site Components:

* Ovn-controller \- manages all networking functions and runs on all network nodes and compute nodes
* Neutron-ovn-metadata-agent \- Handles metadata for OVN networks

Site Components with linuxbridge enabled

* Linuxbridge agent \- manages linuxbridge networks and runs on all network nodes and compute nodes
* DHCP agent \- Manages dhcp for linuxbridge networks, runs on all network nodes.
* Metadata agent \- Manages metadata routing for all linuxbridge networks

![][image5]

### ​2.2.5​ Metrics Service {#​2.2.5​ metrics-service}

The Nectar Metrics Service collects metrics on cloud resources and stores them centrally for users and administrators to query and generate reports. Metrics collection is provided by OpenStack Ceilometer and storage and query APIs are provided by the Gnocchi Project.

The compute agent runs on each compute node and collects information about running virtual machines.

![][image6]

### 2.2.6​ User Proxy Service {#2.2.6​ user-proxy-service}

This service provides users access to the novnc service using haproxy and keepalived. It also provides HA access to glance api for the computes and controllers.

Glance api should not be open to the Internet, it is on this proxy as opposed to the admin proxy because the traffic is user traffic however users should communicate with the core services glance api servers.

### 2.2.7 Admin Proxy Service {#2.2.7-admin-proxy-service}

This service provides Coreservices access to mysql and rabbitmq using haproxy and keepalived.
They also act as the routers for the underlay network.

### ​2.2.8 Relational Database {#​2.2.8 relational-database}

Each nova compute cell requires a relational database. This is provided by a 3 node MariaDB Galera cluster. This cluster needs to be accessible by your nova-conductors and also Core Services nova-conductors

### ​2.2.9​ Message Queue {#​2.2.9​ message-queue}

Each nova compute cell requires a message queue. This is provided by a 3 node RabbitMQ cluster. This cluster needs to be accessible by your nova-conductors and also Core Services nova-conductors. It is also used by the Metrics Service to store all events and polling samples before being collected by Core Services.

### ​2.2.10​ Monitoring Host {#​2.2.10​ monitoring-host}

Each site should run a monitoring host running nagios as a minimum. The nagios config is automatically generated via puppet.

### ​2.2.11​ Logging Host {#​2.2.11​ logging-host}

Each site should run a logging host, this should collect all the logs from the sites machines. It can store these to disk and/or send them to an Elastic Stack (ELK) cluster. It is also used to send some logs to core services which enables faster root cause analysis.

## ​2.3​ Optional Services {#​2.3​ optional-services}

There are several optional services that sites can run.

### ​2.3.1​ Object Store Service {#​2.3.1​ object-store-service}

The Object Store service in the Nectar cloud uses the OpenStack Swift project to provide a HTTP API to store and retrieve files (objects). The service is operated as a national federated Swift cluster across multiple Sites. User requests are made to a proxy server which then retrieves or stores the required object from(to) one of the storage servers in the Swift cluster. Three copies of the object are stored within the cluster to provide fault tolerance. Proxy servers are made highly available by utilising Anycast to direct clients to their closest available endpoint. This also helps to reduce latency and improve transfer speeds for users.

Each member of the Swift cluster must have at least one proxy server and at least three storage servers.

![][image7]

The Object Store service provides storage for the virtual machine images in the Openstack Glance image repository, as well as storage for user data, so it is a crucial component of the federated Research Cloud. So although it is optional for any individual Site to run Swift, it is required that some of the Sites (ideally at least three) run Swift in order to provide the Object Store service for the Nectar Research Cloud.

Any Site can also create their own local Swift service that is separate from the national federated Swift cluster. They can register their local Swift in the same Nectar Keystone and users can use it in the same way by selecting a specific region, although some work is required by the Site to restrict access to their local users.

### ​2.3.2​ Shared File System Service {#​2.3.2​ shared-file-system-service}

The Nectar Shared File System Service allows users to create shared file systems that can then be mounted to their virtual machines. This is provided by the Openstack Manila project and NFS, CIFS and CEPHFS are supported protocols.

![][image8]

## ​2.4​ Standard Operating Environment {#​2.4​ standard-operating-environment}

Core Services prescribes a Standard Operating Environment (SOE) which sites need to adhere to. The SOE is designed to provide a balance of flexibility to a site and a standard experience for both users of the cloud and operators of the cloud.

Operating System: Ubuntu LTS (current version is 22.04)
Cloud platform: OpenStack
Configuration Management: Puppet

All configuration is done via Core Services’ puppet server. Core Services provides a puppet role for each type of server that is to be used alongside site specific puppet roles to configure each server.

Openstack and Ubuntu have a standard upgrade cycle that the Nectar Research Cloud aims to follow fairly closely with an upgrade cycle that is specified by Core Services, which Sites need to conform to, as specified in the OLA.

## ​2.5​ Networking {#​2.5​ networking}

The table below lists the networks needed at each site

| Name | Address range | Mandatory | Comments |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Provider | public | Yes | Use by Floating IPs and Classic Network |
| ServiceManagement | private | Yes | 1 IP for each host |
| Storage | private | No | Usually on all compute nodes and storage system. |
| WAGNET | private | Yes | 1 IP for each compute, network node and admin proxy |
| EndpointAdmin Public | public | Yes | Must be able support MTU 9000 to the internet. To be used by the user/admin proxies (6 IP addresses) May need more for other optional services. |

There are two network drivers in use by the Nectar Cloud.

* Linux bridge: Initially used for provider networks but will be phased out
*

OVN is: uUsed for all the project provisioned networks, routers, floating IPs, firewalls and load balancers. Also used for new provider networks

![][image9]

### ​2.5.1​ User Networking {#​2.5.1​ user-networking}

Each virtual machine must have the ability to be accessible via a publicly routable IP address (IPv4 or IPv6). The number of public IP addresses needs to be proportionate to the number of expected VM instances at the site. From current rates, this value is approximately 1 publicly routable IP per 4 VCPUs.

In the Nectar cloud there are 3 categories of networks that users interact with: provider networks; project networks; and floating IP networks.

#### ​2.5.1.1​ Provider Networks {#​2.5.1.1​ provider-networks}

A provider network is a network that is created by an operator. It can be shared, meaning all projects can use it, or it can be configured to allow one or many projects access. Each site should have one publicly routed provider network. This network will be the default network that a port is created within and bound to an instance if no specific network is chosen at boot time. Networks can span multiple compute cells, they should ideally not span availability zones to provide some level of segregation. They should have no inbound or outbound restrictions and should be located outside institutional firewalls and be non trusted. Subnets can be added to a provider network as utilisation grows allowing for flexibility when scaling.

#### ​2.5.1.2​ Project Networks {#​2.5.1.2​ project-networks}

A project network is a network that has been created by an individual project. These networks are isolated to the project and all traffic is internal to that project. Project networks can span availability zones. A project network can be used with higher level networking services like routers, load balancers and firewalls.

#### ​2.5.1.3​ Floating IP Networks {#​2.5.1.3​ floating-ip-networks}

A floating IP network is created by an operator and is publicly routable. It works in conjunction with project networks to allow cloud external access. They should have no inbound or outbound restrictions and should be located outside institutional firewalls and be non trusted. Use subnets of /24 as the pool of addresses for floating IP networks and add more as required. If the site is only using OVN this network can be the same (and is recommended to be the same) as the provider network.

### ​2.5.2​ Accessibility {#​2.5.2​ accessibility}

For User networking there should be minimal barriers to cross-institutional access to cloud sites. Sites are expected to:

* Have minimal or no institutional firewall to allow the widest access to Australian researchers;
* Allow access directly between Openstack and the site hardware without an intervening firewall, since Openstack contains its own firewall capabilities; and
* Have no human interaction for port and IP configuration, as this is handled by Openstack.

### ​2.5.3​ Operations Networking {#​2.5.3​ operations-networking}

A number of networks are required at a site with various functions. Below describes best practice, however it is also possible to double up if required, e.g. your services and storage network could be the same.

#### ​2.5.3.1​ ServiceManagement Network {#​2.5.3.1​ servicemanagement-network}

A private network attached to all hosts and used by all services to talk to one another.
All database and RabbitMQ traffic should be over this network. It should also be used for live and cold migrations between compute nodes.
Required Minimum 10G network, 9000 MTU

#### ​2.5.3.2​ Storage Network {#​2.5.3.2​ storage-network}

A private network attached to all compute nodes and cinder nodes, It could also potentially be used by glance nodes to use for a cache.
Required Minimum 10G network, 9000 MTU

#### ​2.5.3.3 WAGNET Network {#​2.5.3.3-wagnet-network}

A private VLAN attached to all compute and network nodes. Used for geneve tunnels for project networks across the federation. Only VLAN is needed. No routers necessary. IP range will be assigned by Core Services.
Required Minimum 10G network, 9000 MTU

#### ​2.5.3.4 EndpointAdmin Public Network {#​2.5.3.4-endpointadmin public-network}

Recommended Minimum 10G network to the primary AARNet connection. A secondary geographically diverse link to AARNet is also recommended.

The network paths to AARNet should not traverse any firewalls or security devices which may affect performance and access.

A public network used by Admin and User Proxies, and WAGNET Routers.
Should allow inbound:

* Port 443 for nova vnc open to 0.0.0.0/0
* Port 3306 for mysql open to core services ranges
* Port 5671 for rabbitmq open to core services ranges
* GENEVE for WAGNET routers to core services and other sites' ranges

## ​2.6​ Virtual Infrastructure {#​2.6​ virtual-infrastructure}

To run many of the control infrastructure services for the cloud a number of virtual machines are required. Each service is designed to be highly available with the ability to lose one of the members and have no interruption of service. It is therefore expected that each virtual machine will be hosted on different hardware to ensure hardware failures have limited user impact on the service. Virtual infrastructure is recommended over physical infrastructure to ease upgrades. The exception to this is the Database and Message Queue servers which could run on physical infrastructure if desired.

#### ​2.6.0.1​ Note on Upgrades {#​2.6.0.1​ note-on-upgrades}

The Nectar Research Cloud is upgraded roughly every 6 months following the OpenStack upgrade release cycle. All OpenStack controllers are upgraded using a blue-green deployment strategy so it is essential that you have spare capacity for double the amount of a service during upgrade time. Non openstack services are upgraded in place and the operating system is upgraded every 2 years following the Ubuntu LTS release cycle.

#### ​2.6.0.2​ Containers {#​2.6.0.2​ containers}

The majority of openstack control services use docker containers.

### ​2.6.1​ OpenStack {#​2.6.1​ openstack}

All controllers below are specified on a per cell level. This is not a strict requirement if you have a small cell you can share certain things like DB and MQ servers. Nova MUST have separate controllers per cell. Monitoring and logging hosts should be done at a site level.

#### ​2.6.1.Openstack Controllers {#​2.6.1.openstack-controllers}

2 virtual machines
Services: nova-conductor, nova-novncproxy, cinder-volume, cinder-backup, glance-api
Specs: 8 VCPU, 16GB RAM 30GB Disk
Networking: management

#### ​2.6.1.2 Network Controllers {#​2.6.1.2 network-controllers}

2 virtual machines
Services: ovn-controller, ovn-relay
Specs: 8 VCPU, 16GB RAM 30GB Disk
Networking: management, underlay, public-user

#### ​2.6.1.3​ Admin Proxies {#​2.6.1.3​ admin-proxies}

2 virtual machines
Services: haproxy, keepalived, frr
Specs: 4 VCPU, 8GB RAM 30GB Disk
Networking: management, public-admin, underlay

#### ​2.6.1.4​ User Proxies {#​2.6.1.4​ user-proxies}

2 virtual machines
Services: haproxy, keepalived
Specs: 2 VCPU, 4GB RAM 30GB Disk
Networking: management, public-admin

### ​2.6.2​ Supporting Infrastructure {#​2.6.2​ supporting-infrastructure}

#### ​2.6.2.1​ Relational Database {#​2.6.2.1​ relational-database}

3 virtual machines
Services: MariaDB Galera
Specs: 8 VCPU, 16GB RAM 60GB Disk
Networking: management

#### ​2.6.2.2​ Message Queue {#​2.6.2.2​ message-queue}

3 virtual machines
Services: RabbitMQ
Specs: 8 VCPU, 16GB RAM 30GB Disk
Networking: management

#### ​2.6.2.4​ Monitoring Host {#​2.6.2.4​ monitoring-host}

1 virtual machine
Services: nagios
Specs: 4 VCPU, 8GB RAM 10GB Disk
Networking: management, \[public\]

#### ​2.6.2.5​ Logging Host {#​2.6.2.5​ logging-host}

1 virtual machine
Services: rsyslogd
Specs: 4 VCPU, 8GB RAM 10GB Disk
Storage for storing logs, min 2TB
Networking: management, \[public\]

## ​2.7​ Physical Infrastructure {#​2.7​ physical-infrastructure}

### ​2.7.1​ Control Plane Nodes {#​2.7.1​ control-plane-nodes}

Three physical servers to run all the control infrastructure at the site.
Reference specs (example): 20 cores (2.1GHz), 164GB RAM, 400GB usable disk space (2\*SSD in RAID 1\) per server.

### ​2.7.2​ Compute Nodes {#​2.7.4​ network-nodes}

There is no fixed specification for compute nodes, allowing for flexibility in design, however ARDC will work with cloud sites to develop some reference specifications. All general purpose compute nodes must be able to efficiently support the latest standard [Nectar flavours](https://support.ehelp.edu.au/support/solutions/articles/6000205341-nectar-flavors). In consideration of node density and to not over-burden a node (e.g. network IO, disk IO), a guide to specifications might look to limit nodes to support \~100 VM instances at a typical size of 4 vCPUs/8GB RAM.

In determining the hardware configuration requirements for the site, a guideline for the VCPU to RAM ratio is: 2-3 GB of RAM for each VCPU, since standard flavors have 2GB RAM per VCPU and large RAM flavors have 4GB RAM per VCPU. Hyper-threading should be turned on and overcommit of around 2 is expected. For example; a 10 physical core machine has 20 threads and would support 40 VCPUS and so would need 160GB of RAM available for instances. You should reserve around 8-16GB of RAM for system functions.

Special Purpose compute nodes such as GPU, large RAM, fast IO or other specialised hardware can be supported as nationally funded infrastructure, however these must be justified by requirements from nationally prioritised projects. Some sites already provide some of this hardware using non-standard flavours. ARDC may work with these sites to develop more standardised approaches for supporting these specialised compute nodes in the Nectar cloud.

### ​2.7.3​ Compute Storage {#​2.7.4​ network-nodes}

Storage for virtual machines is provided by both nova and cinder. Each nova flavor has a predefined size of disk. For all current generation flavors this is 30GB. Cinder can also provide block storage for a virtual machine in any size chosen by the user.

Guidelines for capacity and IOPS are 50GB with 10 sequential IOPS per VCPU for root drives and 100GB with 2 IOPS per VCPU for volume storage. So, if a Site has capacity for 1000 VCPUs, it should enable a minimum usable capacity of 50TB for root drives capable of 10,000 IOPS and 100TB for volume storage capable of 2,000 IOPS.

Storage for compute instances can be done several ways, using either local or shared storage, and all have their pros and cons.

Local Storage
Multiple disks are located inside a compute node and RAID is used for performance and redundancy.
Pros: Simple, limited contention
Cons: hard to know how much storage needed. Not flexible.

Shared Storage
Dedicated storage nodes supply storage to instances over the network.
Example using CEPH or some other shared storage solution like NFS.
Pros: Flexible and easier to grow storage separately to compute. Quicker recovery of instances for dead hypervisors.
Cons: More operational overhead. Bigger blast radius, all instances affected when shared storage fails.

To test expected IOPS for a VM , use a tool like fio and run the following example on a compute node

`fio --rw=write --ioengine=sync --fdatasync=1 --directory=test-data --size=22m --bs=2300 --name=mytest`

If the 99th percentile of this test is less than 10ms, you have fairly fast storage.

### ​2.7.4​ Network Nodes {#​2.7.4​ network-nodes}

Two servers to be used for north south traffic for project networks.  Also runs ovn-relay service and keepalived to make ovn-relay HA. Also will run DHCP servers for provider networks.

NOTE: Network nodes can also run as virtual machines.

Reference architecture:
8-16 cores
32-64G RAM
2\*240G disks RAID1 for operating system
2\*25G NICs or 4\*10G NICs

### ​2.7.5​ Object Store Proxy Nodes {#​2.7.5​ object-store-proxy-nodes}

Swift proxy nodes require significant amounts of bandwidth available, recommend at least 3 times the bandwidth of a single storage node. The Proxy is very I/O focused, and this should be maximised.
Reference Architecture:
8 Cores, 32GB RAM, 120G usable storage for operating system.
Publicly routable network with 9000 MTU

### ​2.7.6​ Object Store Storage Nodes {#​2.7.6​ object-store-storage-nodes}

OpenStack Object Storage specifically is designed to run on commodity hardware. The smaller the storage node, the better the redundant distribution and performance can be.  For the data storage arrays of disks of the storage nodes, ensure that all disks are presented as separate physical disks – please note that RAID is not required and not a supported configuration.

Reference Architecture:
1 thread per disk, \~4GB RAM per disk
2 fast disks for account and container storage, 24 larger capacity disks for object storage
Publicly routable network with 9000 MTU

Example spec:
CPU: Intel Xeon Silver 4114, 2.2GHx 10cores/20Threads, 2 sockets
Memory: 12x 8GB RDIMM
Disks: 2x480G SSD(OS drives raid 1
24x8TB 7.2k SAS swift drives
2x 800GB Nvme swift drives
Network card: 25GbE dual port(data)
1x i350 1Gb (in-band management)

216TB usable for a 3 node cluster with 3x replication

# ​3​ Acceptance Testing {#​3​ acceptance-testing}

As part of the acceptance testing for a new Site or AZ, ARDC Core Services runs a series of standard OpenStack compliance tests to ensure it is working correctly. Testing is done using the Tempest Openstack Project. AZs with infrastructure funded by ARDC must pass all acceptance tests in order to federate with the Nectar Research Cloud.

## 3.1 Compute Service {#3.1-compute-service}

### ​3.1.1 Functional tests {#​3.1.1 functional-tests}

* A server instance’s assigned hostname is set to the server name as defined in Nova `(test_create_server.ServersTestJSON.test_host_name_is_same_as_server_name)`
* A server instance has the expected number of VCPUs available `(test_create_server.ServersTestJSON.test_verify_created_server_vcpus)`
* A server instance appears in the server list `(test_create_server.ServersTestJSON.test_list_servers_with_detail)`
* A server details include expected information (Image) `(test_create_server.ServersTestJSON.test_verify_server_details)`
* A server instance lists image details correctly when using a volume `(test_create_server.ServersTestBootFromVolume.test_verify_server_details)`
* The API sets up and returns a VNC console `(test_server_actions.ServerActionsTestJSON.test_get_vnc_console)`
* A volume can be attached and the instance then rebuilt `(test_server_actions.ServerActionsTestJSON.test_rebuild_server_with_volume_attached)`
* A server can be resized
  `(test_server_actions.ServerActionsTestJSON.test_resize_server_confirm)`
* A server can be cold migrated from one hypervisor to another `(test_migrations.MigrationsAdminTest.test_cold_migration)`
* A server can be live migrated from one hypervisor to another, with block migration `(test_live_migration.LiveMigrationTest.test_live_block_migration)`

## 3.2 Volume service {#3.2 volume-service}

### 3.2.1 Functional Tests {#3.2.1-functional-tests}

* A volume can be created, then attached and detached to a server instance under various power states
  `(test_attach_volume.AttachVolumeTestJSON.test_attach_detach_volume, test_volumes_actions.VolumesActionsTest.test_attach_detach_volume_to_instance)`
* A server instance can be booted from a volume `(test_volumes_actions.VolumesActionsTest.test_volume_bootable)`
* A volume can be extended `(test_volumes_extend.VolumesExtendTest.test_volume_extend)`
* A volume snapshot can support all operations `(test_volumes_snapshots.VolumesSnapshotTest.test_snapshot_create_get_list_update_delete)`
* A volume can be backed up using the force flag, while in attached or in-use state `(test_volumes_backup.VolumesBackupsTest.test_backup_create_attached_volume)`

## ​3.3 Networking service {#​3.3 networking-service}

### 3.3.1 Functional Tests {#3.3.1-functional-tests}

* Various advanced network functions work as intended, including attaching a network to an instance and testing external and internal network connectivity with floating IPs
  `(test_network_basic_ops.TestNetworkBasicOps.test_network_basic_ops)`

# ​4​ Data Centre Environment {#​4​ data-centre-environment}

Sites are to provide a modern data centre facility for the housing of the cloud infrastructure components. It is recommended that this is at least a Tier 2 Data Centre as defined by Uptime Institute.

# ​5​ Service recovery {#​5​ service-recovery}

Sites will be required to implement a service recovery procedure and provide a documented statement of the processes and procedures.

The specifics of how the service recovery process is implemented will be at the Sites’ discretion, however some critical components must be included.

* Database
  * The OpenStack databases must be backed up on (at least) a nightly basis
  * It is recommended that backups are stored both on and off-site
  * Database recovery procedure should be tested regularly
* Control Plane Hardware
  * Must have vendor maintenance or standby hardware in case of failure and should be recoverable by the Next Business Day (NBD)
* Storage
  * Must have vendor maintenance or standby hardware in case of failure and should be recoverable by the NBD
* Network
  * All network components should be fully duplicated to provide no single point of failure.
* Configuration
  * All configuration details must be stored in version control with a remote copy
* Data Centre Environment
  * Recovery processes for power and cooling failures
